abstract:
This chapter examines the historical evolution of central–local government relations in Japan through the lenses of administration, finance, and politics. By utilizing a conceptual framework of "interfusion" versus "separation," the analysis clarifies the unique structural characteristics of Japanese governance. During its modernization in the Meiji era, Japan looked to continental European models and adopted an interfusion system, in which the central government delegates the implementation of national policies to local authorities rather than executing them through its own local branches. This relationship has proven remarkably resilient despite shifting political landscapes: it survived the functional centralization of the wartime regime, the institutional decentralization reforms introduced during the post-WWII Occupation, and the more recent wave of decentralization reforms starting in the 1990s. 

The study highlights how the interfusion model has been consistently reinforced through large-scale municipal mergers and the establishment of a robust fiscal adjustment system. While Japan stands out internationally for its high ratio of local government activity relative to total government spending, the chapter argues that this is fundamentally supported by the deeply integrated nature of the central–local relationship. Despite contemporary challenges such as demographic decline and crisis management, this interfused structure remains a defining pillar of the Japanese state.
